content.text: AI Risk Taxonomy and Enterprise Risk Register

An enterprise AI risk register is a structured inventory of the risks that arise from deploying Claude across the organisation. It serves three purposes: it forces systematic enumeration of what can go wrong (preventing gaps), it provides a common language for risk owners across legal, security, data, and product teams, and it produces the documentation that regulators and auditors expect to see when evaluating an organisation's AI governance maturity.

attrs.alt: AI risk taxonomy tree: five L1 categories with three example L2 risks each

content.text: The taxonomy above organises enterprise AI risks into five top-level categories. Each category maps to a different ownership domain within the enterprise: data and privacy risks are owned by the DPO, model quality risks by the AI product team, operational risks by platform engineering, legal risks by the compliance function, and reputational risks by a cross-functional AI ethics board.

Each entry in the risk register is a structured record with standardised fields. Consistency across fields enables heat maps, ownership reports, and compliance evidence packages to be generated programmatically from the register.
Data & Privacy | Model Quality | Operational | Legal | Reputational
Short name of the risk, e.g. 'PII leakage via RAG output'
What can go wrong and why
Existing mitigations (e.g. output redaction, consent gate)
Role responsible for managing this risk

The residual score (after controls) drives prioritisation: risks with a residual score above 12 on a 25-point scale (5×5) require active mitigation projects. Risks with accepted status have been formally signed off by the risk owner as within the organisation's stated risk appetite.
Sample Risk Register for Claude Deployments
Output redaction, PII scanner, sampling validation
Grounding via RAG, confidence thresholds, human review
Consent capture, audit trail, preference center
Content filters, human-in-loop for sensitive domains

Storing the risk register as a YAML or JSON file in version control brings several governance benefits: changes are attributed to specific team members, diffs make before-and-after comparisons trivial during audits, and CI pipelines can enforce schema validation so that entries are never added without mandatory fields.

A 5×5 risk heat map visualises the distribution of residual risks and immediately shows whether the portfolio is clustering near the tolerable zone or whether high-residual risks are accumulating without mitigation projects.

def generate_heat_map_data(entries: list[RiskEntry]) -> dict:
    """
    Return a 5×5 grid (likelihood × impact) with the risk IDs in each cell.
    Key: (likelihood, impact) → list of risk_ids.
    """
    grid: dict[tuple[int, int], list[str]] = {}
    for e in entries:
        cell = (e.residual_likelihood, e.residual_impact)
        grid.setdefault(cell, []).append(e.risk_id)
    return grid

def print_heat_map(entries: list[RiskEntry]) -> None:
    grid = generate_heat_map_data(entries)
    print("Residual Risk Heat Map (likelihood × impact)")
    print(f"{'':>12}", end="")
    for impact in range(1, 6):
        print(f"  I={impact}  ", end="")
    print()
    for likelihood in range(5, 0, -1):
        print(f"  L={likelihood}        ", end="")
        for impact in range(1, 6):
            cell = grid.get((likelihood, impact), [])
            score = likelihood * impact
            marker = "■■■" if score >= 15 else ("■■ " if score >= 8 else "■  ")
            print(f" {marker}({len(cell)}) ", end="")
        print()

A residual score of 15 or above (top-right quadrant of the heat map) should trigger an escalation to the AI Steering Committee. The committee decides whether to invest in additional controls, transfer the risk (cyber insurance, contractual limitation), or formally accept it with documented sign-off.
Quarterly: re-score all open risks; update control effectiveness based on incident data and validation metrics.
On every new Claude deployment or feature launch: add new risks before go-live, not after.
After any AI-related incident: add a post-incident risk entry and close or update the causal risk if it already exists.
Annually: full taxonomy review — check whether emerging risk categories (e.g., agentic AI autonomy, multi-model pipelines) require new L1 categories.
Before regulatory audits: export the register to the required format (NIST AI RMF Govern function, ISO 42001 Annex A) and confirm all accepted risks have documented sign-off.

A risk register without a risk appetite statement has no basis for deciding which residual scores are acceptable. The appetite statement defines the thresholds that separate tolerated risk from risk that requires active treatment:

# Risk appetite thresholds for Claude enterprise deployments
RISK_APPETITE = {
    "data_privacy":    {"accept_below": 6,  "escalate_above": 12},
    "model_quality":   {"accept_below": 8,  "escalate_above": 15},
    "operational":     {"accept_below": 9,  "escalate_above": 15},
    "legal":           {"accept_below": 6,  "escalate_above": 10},
    "reputational":    {"accept_below": 5,  "escalate_above": 10},
}

def classify_residual(entry: "RiskEntry") -> str:
    """
    Classify a risk entry against the appetite thresholds.
    Returns: "tolerable" | "review" | "escalate"
    """
    appetite = RISK_APPETITE.get(entry.category, {"accept_below": 8, "escalate_above": 15})
    score = entry.residual_score
    if score <= appetite["accept_below"]:
        return "tolerable"
    if score >= appetite["escalate_above"]:
        return "escalate"
    return "review"

def appetite_report(entries: list["RiskEntry"]) -> dict:
    """Summarise register against appetite thresholds."""
    from collections import Counter
    counts = Counter(classify_residual(e) for e in entries)
    return dict(counts)

Data privacy and legal categories carry lower appetite thresholds than operational categories because regulatory penalties and reputational damage from a data breach typically exceed the cost of a service disruption. This asymmetry is deliberate and should be documented in the appetite statement itself.
Risk Register Reporting to the AI Steering Committee

The AI Steering Committee (or equivalent governance body) needs a concise summary view of the register at each quarterly meeting, not the full YAML. Generate a report that highlights changes since the last review: new risks, risks whose scores changed, and risks that breached the escalation threshold.
Mapping to NIST AI RMF and ISO 42001

Enterprise AI risk registers increasingly need to demonstrate alignment with recognised frameworks. The NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0) and ISO/IEC 42001 (AI Management System) both require documented risk identification and treatment processes that the register directly supports.
owner, status, review_date; register is the policy artefact
category, title, description, likelihood, impact, inherent_score
residual_likelihood, residual_impact, residual_score; heat map data
controls list; quarterly review updates; incident-triggered entries

When preparing for an ISO 42001 audit, export the risk register filtered to status='open' AND residual_score > 8 and attach it as evidence for Clause 6.1 (Actions to address risks and opportunities). The audit trail of quarterly score changes satisfies Clause 9.1 (Monitoring, measurement, analysis and evaluation).
Integrating Risk Scores with the Deployment Gate

Risk register entries for a specific feature can feed directly into the deployment gate — preventing a Claude-powered feature from going live if its associated risks have not been mitigated below the appetite threshold.

def deployment_gate(feature_id: str, entries: list["RiskEntry"]) -> tuple[bool, list[str]]:
    """
    Return (can_deploy, blocking_reasons).
    A feature is blocked if any associated open risk is classified as 'escalate'.
    """
    feature_risks = [e for e in entries if feature_id in e.risk_id and e.status == "open"]
    blocking = [
        f"{e.risk_id} ({e.title}): residual={e.residual_score}, status={classify_residual(e)}"
        for e in feature_risks
        if classify_residual(e) == "escalate"
    ]
    return (len(blocking) == 0), blocking


# Example usage
entries = load_register()
can_deploy, blockers = deployment_gate("AI-DATA-001", entries)
if not can_deploy:
    print("Deployment blocked due to unmitigated risks:")
    for b in blockers:
        print(f"  - {b}")

An enterprise AI risk register organises Claude deployment risks into five taxonomy categories (data & privacy, model quality, operational, legal, reputational), captures each risk with standardised fields including likelihood, impact, controls, and residual score, and stores the register as a versioned YAML file that CI can validate for schema compliance. Heat map generation surfaces the residual risk distribution to the steering committee, and a structured review cadence ensures the register reflects current control effectiveness rather than becoming stale documentation.
